title: A Truth Known to Everyone!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, party-name, punctuation, and word-break errors have been repaired and non-text scan fragments removed. Historical claims and press quotations remain attributed. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Today's Daily Thanthi, 24 March 2014, carries on its front page this headline over Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's speech: “If Karunanidhi opposed kar seva, why did he ally with the Bharatiya Janata Party?” The Chief Minister has asked; Daily Thanthi has published it. Can I leave it unanswered? I shall answer. You may recognise the truth yourself.

Speaking in Thoothukudi, Jayalalithaa said the AIADMK had never sent anyone for kar seva. I had already replied in detail, date by date. I quoted Dinamani's report of 24 November 1992 on her speech at the National Integration Council in Delhi. Its very headline was “Kar Seva: Jayalalithaa Insists.” She said: “This National Integration Council must take the decision necessary to create an appropriate situation for kar seva. Every step must be taken to approach the courts for permission. Conditions must be created for kar seva on the site acquired by the Uttar Pradesh government.” Having said this then, how can she now say she did not support it? I also referred to posters put up by the Tamil Nadu Vishwa Hindu Parishad thanking her.

When she denied sending people, I cited The Times of India. It reported the Uttar Pradesh VHP secretary as saying Chief Minister Jayalalithaa had sent more than one thousand AIADMK members from Tamil Nadu to the kar seva at Ayodhya and promised further assistance. Theekkathir reproduced that report in a box on 7 December 1992. I asked: is what I say true, or is Jayalalithaa's denial true?
I also cited a report in which A.K.A. Abdul Samad, the Member of Parliament and cherished friend of Muslims, said AIADMK members participated. Despite all this, she repeats the same denial.

At Cuddalore she has now asked how the DMK could ally with the BJP, which supported kar seva. The kar seva occurred on 6 December 1992; the DMK allied with the BJP in 1999, seven years later. Yet I am ready to explain even that.

Having contested and won the 1991 election with Congress, Jayalalithaa conveniently forgets that within a year she supported the BJP on kar seva. On the eve of Prime Minister Narasimha Rao's visit to Tamil Nadu on 1 December 1992, she told Dinamani editor Prabhu Chawla that there was a generational gap between herself and Narasimha Rao when asked how he differed from Rajiv Gandhi.

The primary reason the DMK had to establish a relationship with the BJP in 1999 was Jayalalithaa herself. As I have explained, while the AIADMK participated in Vajpayee's 1998 government, she demanded that he dismiss the DMK government in Tamil Nadu. The BJP and Vajpayee refused until the end. She called Home Minister Advani “anti-national” and abused BJP leader L. Ganesan for greeting me at my platinum-jubilee celebration.

On 28 July 1998, Prime Minister Vajpayee invited me, as Chief Minister, to Delhi on 6 August to discuss implementing the Cauvery Tribunal's interim award. I convened an all-party meeting on 3 August. Every party favoured attending except the AIADMK. The Delhi meeting produced the important decision that 205 TMC of water specified in the interim award must be released. While party leaders and newspapers praised it, Jayalalithaa alone said Vajpayee had fallen for Karunanidhi's trick and rejected what she called their agreement.

When reporters questioned Vajpayee at the MDMK's Anna birthday rally, he said the future would determine whether the AIADMK was a stable and trustworthy ally; that the BJP would accept support from parties such as the DMK if offered; that no discussions had occurred; and that Karunanidhi had said he was prepared to support the BJP if it proved secular.

After the AIADMK withdrew support, Vajpayee moved a confidence motion in the Lok Sabha on 16 April 1999. DMK parliamentary leader Murasoli Maran said Indira Gandhi had formed her minority government with the help of twenty-five DMK MPs and Communist members; abolished privy purses and nationalised banks with DMK support; and that during the Emergency the DMK, BJP and Marxists became prison companions. Throughout its history, he said, the DMK had protected oppressed people and minorities and reaffirmed that commitment.

Maran continued: “Until yesterday I opposed this government. Apart from its credit for resolving the Cauvery problem, I cannot praise its achievements. But the attempt being made with the support of our AIADMK friends is against the interests of the State and nation. We therefore support the continuation of the present government.”

Asked about it, I said that describing communalism as less cruel than Jayalalithaa's corruption did not make communalism sweet. If one calls a person worse than a snake, does that make the snake good? It still has venom.

I added: “The DMK will always be a fortress protecting minorities. We yield to no one in eliminating religious fanaticism; that principle is in our bloodstream. We did not compromise with BJP policy in making this decision. The DMK's principles remain intact, and we have an enduring duty to protect them without loss or dispersal. At the same time, we believe we must hold a firm position in India's politics and democracy. Personally, I have a good opinion of Vajpayee and have often called him a good man.”
The DMK General Council met on 2 June 1999 and debated the matter at length before resolving to ally with the BJP. That same resolution affirmed continuing work for minority communities.

Udanpirappē, this is the answer to Jayalalithaa's Cuddalore question. Just as she kept the Communist parties trusting her until the end and then cut their throats, she allied with the BJP in 1998, won, joined its government, tormented the Prime Minister and Home Minister, withdrew support and brought the government down. Had she not done so, no situation requiring a DMK-BJP alliance would have arisen. After she overthrew the BJP government and left it without support, the DMK joined that front out of a humane consideration and only with a written assurance that minorities would suffer no harm.

Though the DMK had to ally with the BJP, it never abandoned its basic principles or harassed the BJP as Jayalalithaa did while participating in government. Nor did the DMK ever act in support of kar seva. But Jayalalithaa?

At the Tamil Nadu Muslim Munnetra Kazhagam's Right to Life Conference in Chennai on 4 July 1999, she proclaimed: “I give my Muslim brothers an assurance. I made a mistake earlier. I always have the courage to admit a mistake. To atone for it, I myself brought down the BJP government. The AIADMK will never again ally with the BJP.” Yet in the 2004 parliamentary election she allied with that same BJP and lost.

I am not prepared to ask why she allied with it. It is a truth known to everyone that Jayalalithaa supported kar seva against Muslims and sent people to it. I need not explain it. Every Muslim brother knows that truth. Whatever falsehood Jayalalithaa tells cannot become truth.
With affection,
M.K.
25 March 2014
